# Refined modeling and coupled vibration analysis of the sleeper lifting device in long span bridges under temperature and train dynamics

[*Scientific Reports*](/srep)
**volume 15**, Article number: 11526 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

1463 Accesses

[Metrics details](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

The safety and smoothness of high-speed train operations, particularly through bridge zones, are crucial for ensuring operational stability and comfort. Regions such as large-span continuous and simply supported girder bridges, specifically the beam joint regions and beam ends, pose significant challenges due to the dynamic interaction between trains, tracks, and bridges. This study develops a refined coupled model of the train-track-sleeper lifting device-bridge (TTSB) system to simulate the dynamic responses of trains passing through large-displacement sleeper-lifting device (LSD) zones. The model incorporates the effect of axial displacement changes caused by temperature variations at the bridge girder ends, which influence the spacing of fasteners. The refined modeling approach improves both the accuracy and computational efficiency of dynamic simulations. The methodology employs a numerical model to simulate train dynamics at various speeds (250–425 km/h) and fastener spacing ranges (0.35–0.85 m). The study examines key parameters, including vertical and lateral displacements, accelerations, wheel load reduction rates, and derailment coefficients. The results show that increased fastener spacing leads to significant changes in vertical and lateral displacement and acceleration (up to 45.8% for Fastener A and 43.4% for Fastener B). Additionally, the wheel load reduction rate and derailment coefficient exhibit fluctuations with varying fastener spacing, highlighting safety implications. These findings validate the model’s effectiveness and offer insights for optimizing track structure design in high-speed railways, improving safety and operational stability.

## Introduction

In recent years, the Chinese high-speed railway system has developed rapidly, establishing itself as a global leader in the field of high-speed railways[1](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR1 "Zhai, W. & Zhao, C. Frontiers and challenges of sciences and technologies in modern railway engineering. J. Southwest Jiaotong Univ. 51(02), 209–226 (2016) (Chinese)."). As the Chinese high-speed railway network continues to expand and improve, it has become the preferred mode of transportation for the public. However, this growth has also led to increased demands for safety and comfort during travel.

Bridges are a critical component of high-speed railway systems, and their structural design has seen significant advancements driven by modern technological developments, offering promising prospects for future innovation[2](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR2 "Wang, H. The nonlinear vibration research on large-span bridge structure under turbulent wind. Harbin Institute of Technology (2016) (Chinese)."). Recent trends in bridge construction emphasize longer span lengths to accommodate the need for crossing rivers, canyons, and other obstacles efficiently. The role of bridges in high-speed railway infrastructure has become increasingly prominent. By 2021, bridges accounted for an average of approximately 58% of the total length of high-speed railway lines, with some lines reaching a bridge ratio as high as 94.2%[3](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR3 "Gou, H., Liu, C., Ban, X., Meng, X. & Pu, Q. Research progress of detection, monitoring and running safety of bridge-track system for high-speed railway. J. Traffic Transp. Eng. 22(01), 1–23 (2022) (Chinese).").

The widespread application of bridges in railway transportation has highlighted the importance of addressing dynamic wheel-rail interactions, which often induce coupled vibrations in trains, tracks, and bridges[4](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR4 "Liu, S., Jiang, L., Zhou, W., Yu, J. & Liu, X. Study on the influence of damage characteristics of longitudinal ballastless track on the dynamic performance of train-track-bridge coupled systems. Arch. Civ. Mech. Eng. 23(1), 1–17 (2022)."),[5](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR5 "Zhihao, Z., Shengyang, Z., Yun, Y., Jun, L. & Chengbiao, C. Dynamics analysis of train-track-bridge coupled system considering spatial flexibility of high piers and system longitudinal vibrations. Veh. Syst. Dyn. 61, 2613–2637 (2023)."). These coupled vibrations can significantly affect the operational performance and reliability of the train-track-bridge (TTB) system. Large random dynamic impacts not only reduce the lifespan of bridge structures but also compromise the smoothness and comfort of train operations, potentially leading to serious accidents[6](#ref-CR6 "Su, J. Train-bridge coupling vibration and fatigue analysis of double-layer railway steel truss cable-stayed bridge with six lines. Southwest Jiaotong University (2020) (Chinese)."),[7](#ref-CR7 "Gou, W., Wang, X. & Yu, B. Residual life evaluation of heavy haul railway prestressed concrete T-beams based on train-track-bridge coupling model. J. Central South Univ. (Sci. Technol.) 54(12), 4806–4816 (2023) (Chinese)."),[8](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR8 "Mao, J. Random vibration analysis and application of train-track-bridge systems based on probability density evolution. Central South University (2016) (Chinese)."). In response, researchers have made substantial progress in advancing the theory of coupled random vibration analysis for train-bridge systems. Ping Lou et al.[9](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR9 "Lou, P. & Au, F. T. K. Finite element formulae for internal forces of Bernoulli–Euler beams under moving vehicles. J. Sound Vibr. 332, 1533–1552 (2013).") investigated the influence of finite element model lengths on TTB vibrations. Zhai et al.[10](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR10 "Zhai, W. Theory of Train-Rail-Bridge Dynamic Interaction and Engineering Ethics (Science Press, 2011) (Chinese).") conducted pioneering theoretical and applied research on TTB interactions. Yu et al.[11](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR11 "Yu, Z., Mao, J., Guo, F. & Guo, W. Non-stationary random vibration analysis of a 3D train-bridge system using the probability density evolution method. J. The TTB system is also subject to strong random vibrations due to multi-source random excitations such as track irregularities, temperature variations, earthquakes, and wind loads. These factors necessitate more complex dynamic analyses to ensure safety and performance. Jiang[20](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR20 "Jiang, B. The influence of the alignment change of high speed railway bridge on the dynamic performance of rail vehicle bridge system. Harbin Institute of Technology (2017) (Chinese).") investigated the effects of track deformation on the dynamic performance of the TTB system. Gou et al. [21](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR21 "Gou, H., Gao, H., Ban, X., Meng, X. & Bao, Y. Vibration energy transmission in high-speed train-track-bridge coupled systems. Eng. Struct. 297, 117019 (2023).")developed a TTB interaction coupling model that considers track irregularities, quantitatively analyzing the vibration energy and transmission characteristics of the coupled system. Zhu et al.[22](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR22 "Zhu, Z., Gong, W., Zhou, G., Yang, Y. & Jiang, L. A simplified weak coupling dynamic analysis method for running safety evaluation of train on bridges under earthquake excitation. Eng. Chen et al.[23](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR23 "Chen, Y., Jiang, L., Lai, Z. & Li, J. A novel evaluation of train running safety on the HSR bridges under earthquakes based on the observed track deformation rate. Eng. Jinfeng et al.[24](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR24 "Jinfeng, W., Cai, C. S., Xiaozhen, L. & Dejun, L. Dynamic analysis of train and bridge in crosswinds based on a coupled wind-train-track-bridge model. Adv. Struct. Eng. 26, 904–919 (2023).") established a wind-train-rail-bridge coupling model to study the dynamic responses of train-bridge systems under crosswind conditions, incorporating the vibration effects of track structures. Currently, most academic research focuses on the coupled analysis of TTB systems. However, relatively limited attention has been given to the effects of bridge-end deformations and rail deformations on the overall structural behavior in these models. In practice, large-span bridges, under the combined effects of temperature variations and dynamic loads, often experience significant thermal expansion and contraction at the beam ends. These deformations can increase the spacing between rails and sleepers in the beam joint areas, enlarge the distance between fasteners, and induce rail deformations. Consequently, these changes result in non-uniform stiffness in the track structure foundation, potentially compromising the safety of high-speed train operations. To address these challenges, researchers have investigated several related aspects, including factors influencing beam-end displacements[25](#ref-CR25 "Wang, T. Bridge Constr. 45(06), 41–46 (2015) (Chinese)."),[46](#ref-CR46 "Guo, H. et al. Research on bridge expansion joint of HSR long span steel bridges. Railw. Eng. 60(10), 1–7 (2020) (Chinese)."),[47](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR47 "Tao, L. et al. Experimental and numerical investigation of the expansion mechanism of beam-end expansion devices in large-span bridge. Eng. Struct. 298, 117050 (2024)."). Among these solutions, the LSD has gained significant attention. This device adjusts the relative positions of sleepers, thereby altering the dynamic characteristics of the train-bridge system and alleviating coupling vibrations. By introducing a mechanism for large-displacement adjustments, it optimizes the dynamic interaction between vehicles and bridges, contributing to improved safety and performance.

Kong[48](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR48 "Kong, F. Vertical dynamic characteristic analysis of bridge terminal sleeper-supporting apparatus. J. Shijiazhuang Tiedao Univ. (Nat. Sci.) 22(03), 82–85 (2009) (Chinese).") calculated the vertical dynamic response of a sleeper-lifting device under train loads using nonlinear transient dynamic analysis principles and ANSYS software. The results demonstrated that the device effectively controls vertical rail irregularities at beam joints. Chen et al.[49](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR49 "Chen, X. et al. A type of girder end expansion device for railway large-span bridges (2023) (Chinese).") designed a novel sleeper-lifting device for beam ends of large-span railway bridges. By incorporating an upper-bearing structure with a steel longitudinal beam fixed at the center of the sleeper between the two tracks, their design enhances train safety and reduces maintenance requirements Liu[50](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR50 "Liu, W. Research on dynamic response characteristics and structural optimization design of rail expansion joint on suspension bridges in high-speed railways. Beijing Jiaotong University (2020) (Chinese).") developed a coupled dynamics model of a high-speed railway bridge-stretching sleeper-lifting device-vehicle system using finite element theory and structural dynamics, conducting a detailed investigation into the dynamic behavior of vehicles passing through the device. Gong[51](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR51 "Gong, Y. On sleeper lifting device on the large beam crevice for Weihe Grand Bridge on Xi′an airport line. Urban Mass Transit. 21(07), 58–61 (2018) (Chinese).") proposed a design strategy for sleeper-lifting devices using two or three steel longitudinal beams to address excessive sleeper spacing caused by significant beam-end expansion on the Wei River Bridge. Through finite element analysis, he examined the resonance effects of train frequencies and operating speeds on the device. Cai et al.[52](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR52 "Cai, X. et al. Matching optimization method between sleeper lifting device and track structure of high-speed railway bridge (2019).") provided an optimization method for matching expansion sleeper-lifting devices with track structures in high-speed railway bridges. Despite these advancements, research on LSD has not fully addressed their influencing factors, mechanisms, and optimization design within the context of train-bridge coupled vibration. Moreover, a comprehensive stochastic coupled model of the TTSB system remains undeveloped, leaving gaps in safety and stability analyses. To address these limitations, this study proposes a refined coupled vibration model for the TTSB system, incorporating the effects of a LSD. By analyzing axial deformations of beam ends caused by temperature variations and dynamic loads, the study examines the impact of changes in fastener spacing due to the device on train responses and operational safety. Using the refined model, computational analyses are performed to investigate the coupled vibration characteristics of high-speed trains passing through the LSD. The findings aim to provide valuable insights for improving the design and operational safety of high-speed railways.

## Framework and bridge analysis

### Bridge introduction

The sleeper-lifting device examined in this study is located on a large bridge spanning the Yellow River in a central province of China, which has completed construction[53](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#ref-CR53 "Jin, L. et al. Overall design of Zheng-Ji high-speed railway Yellow River Bridge. Railway Standard Des. 65, 1–5 (2021) (Chinese)."). The bridge is situated in the northeastern part of the province and adopts a shared construction form for both road and railway use, with space reserved for urban railways and expressways. It has a total length of 12.18 km, with the main bridge spanning 2,016 m. The main structure consists of seven 112-m simply supported steel truss girders and one continuous steel truss girder span measuring (112 m + 6 × 168 m + 112 m). The bridge is designed as a double-deck rail-cum-road bridge, featuring four railway tracks on the lower deck and six highway lanes on the upper deck. The initial design specifies a high-speed railway design speed of 350 km/h and employs CRTS I dual-block ballastless track. This study focuses on developing a numerical simulation model of the TTSB system for the main bridge, particularly the (112 m + 6 × 168 m + 112 m) long-span steel truss girder sections, as well as adjacent spans consisting of several simply supported steel truss girder bridges and concrete continuous girder bridges. The schematic diagram of the model is presented in Fig. [1](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#Fig1).

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-025-95884-0/MediaObjects/41598_2025_95884_Fig1_HTML.png)

Schematic diagram of a long-span continuous girder bridge designed for dual use: railway and road. (Software: Microsoft Office PowerPoint; Version: Microsoft Office 2019; URL: <https://www.microsoft.com>).

This paper is based on a real engineering case involving a long-span bridge over the Yellow River in China, analyzing the dynamic response variation patterns of the train-bridge system at the LSD under the combined effects of temperature and train loads. The overall research methodology is illustrated in Fig. [2](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#Fig2), which is divided into four stages to accomplish the refined modeling and analysis.

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-025-95884-0/MediaObjects/41598_2025_95884_Fig2_HTML.png)

Research flowchart outlining the methodology and steps of the proposed work. (Software: Microsoft Office PowerPoint; Version: Microsoft Office 2019; URL: <https://www.microsoft.com>).

In the first stage, a long-span bridge over the Yellow River in China is selected as the modeling sample. Key structural parameters of the bridge are input to establish a surrogate model. In the second stage, the LSD is incorporated into the TTB coupled random vibration numerical model. A refined TTSB numerical model is then established in MATLAB using the bridge’s key structural parameters.

In the third stage, the dynamic changes of detailed components during actual operation are considered, focusing on the impact of the longitudinal deformation of the bridge on the LSD. The effects of temperature and vehicle dynamic loads on the longitudinal displacement of the bridge are analyzed separately. The temperature load analysis is conducted by establishing a Midas model, while the vehicle dynamic load analysis is performed using the TTB coupled random vibration numerical model, incorporating the Chinese high-speed railway ballastless track spectrum and the German low-interference track spectrum. Detailed combinations of working conditions are developed based on longitudinal displacement for varying train speeds and fastener spacings of the LSD. In the fourth stage, the study considers the influence of random multi-source factors and investigates the dynamic response effects and influence patterns of the train-bridge system equipped with the LSD.

This paper aims to deepen the understanding of the structural response effects of the TTSB system under multi-factor influences through response feedback. It also provides scientific references and recommendations for the structural design and optimization of engineering cases, offering significant engineering value for ensuring the safety of high-speed train operations on long-span bridges.

### Analysis of longitudinal deformation of long-span steel truss girder bridges under temperature effect

Displacements and deformations of the track, bridge, and detailed components caused by temperature changes significantly impact the stability and safety of train operations. This section investigates the influence of temperature on the longitudinal deformation of the bridge and explores the impact of spacing variations in LSD due to bridge deformation on the dynamic response of the TTB coupled stochastic vibration. These insights contribute to a better understanding of the coupled stochastic vibration characteristics of the system. To achieve this, a 1:1 finite element model of the main bridge (112 m + 6 × 168 m + 112 m) is established using Midas, with a four-track railway on the lower level and a six-lane highway on the upper level, as shown in Fig. [3](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#Fig3). The Midas model is used to calculate the static and dynamic load stiffness of the long-span steel truss girder bridge. The results serve as a basis for accurately analyzing the train-bridge coupled dynamic characteristics of the Yellow River Grand Bridge under high-speed train conditions in Section “[Analysis of the impact of LSD deformation on train-bridge coupling vibrations](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#Sec10)”.

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-025-95884-0/MediaObjects/41598_2025_95884_Fig3_HTML.png)

Midas finite element model of a long-span steel truss bridge. (Software: Midas Civil; Version: Midas Civil 2022; URL: <https://www.midasit.cn/>).

This section focuses exclusively on exploring the effects of deformation displacement on the sleeper-lifting device, specifically addressing the longitudinal deformation of the long-span bridge caused by temperature variations. Based on climate data for the region where the bridge is located, the extreme highest temperature is recorded at 42.5 °C, and the extreme lowest temperature is − 10.2 °C, with a design temperature of 15 °C. The analysis considers the most unfavorable temperature scenario, assuming a system temperature difference of ± 35 °C. The calculation results, shown in Fig. [4](/articles/s41598-025-95884-0#Fig4), indicate that under a temperature change of ± 35 °C, the maximum axial temperature expansion and contraction at the beam ends of the (112 m + 6 × 168 m + 112 m) continuous steel truss girder is 220 mm, which aligns with the original design value.

![figure 4](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-025-95884-0/MediaObjects/41598_2025_95884_Fig4_HTML.png)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Research highlights critical interactions between train dynamics, track irregularities, and bridge structures, revealing complex coupled vibrations affecting operational stability and safety.
Increased train speeds significantly amplify dynamic responses, leading to higher vibration levels and greater derailment risks, particularly in transition and viaduct sections.
Analysis shows specific vibration amplification zones within viaducts, with maximum amplitudes observed at critical speeds, emphasizing the need for robust track and bridge designs.
